title: 2 and 3 factor decomposition
text:
library(tibble)

mydat <- tribble(
  ~pop,~psq,~pgr,
  "P1", 5/15, 3/5,
  "P2", 10/15, 8/10,
) 
mydat

# A tibble: 2 × 3
  pop     psq   pgr
  <chr> <dbl> <dbl>
1 P1    0.333   0.6
2 P2    0.667   0.8




library(DasGuptR)


Attaching package: 'DasGuptR'


The following object is masked from 'package:datasets':

    uspop

dgres <- dgnpop(x = mydat,
       pop = "pop",
       factors = c("psq","pgr"))


DG decomposition being conducted with R = psq*pgr

head(dgres)

  rate pop std.set factor
1 0.20  P1    <NA>  crude
2 0.53  P2    <NA>  crude
3 0.23  P1      P2    psq
4 0.47  P2      P1    psq
5 0.30  P1      P2    pgr
6 0.40  P2      P1    pgr

)

  <chr> <dbl> <dbl>
1 P1    0.333   0.6
2 P2    0.667   0.8




library(DasGuptR)

dgres <- dgnpop(x = mydat,
       pop = "pop",
       factors = c("psq","pgr"))


DG decomposition being conducted with R = psq*pgr

dg_table(dgres)

        P1   P2 diff decomp
psq   0.23 0.47 0.23     70
pgr   0.30 0.40 0.10     30
crude 0.20 0.53 0.33    100

dgres <- dgnpop(x = mydat,
       pop = "pop",
       factors = c("pcol","psq","pgr"))


DG decomposition being conducted with R = pcol*psq*pgr

dg_table(dgres)

pgr   0.34 0.37 0.023    6.9
crude 0.20 0.53 0.333  100.0

title: 2 and 3 factor decomposition
text:
mydat <- tribble(
  ~pop, ~shape,    ~pshp, ~pgreen,
  1,    "circle",  10/24,  2/10,
  1,    "square",  10/24,  3/10,
  1,    "triangle", 4/24,  3/4,
  2,    "circle",   7/24,  2/7,
  2,    "square",   7/24,  4/7,
  2,    "triangle",10/24,  8/10,
)
mydat

# A tibble: 6 × 4
    pop shape     pshp pgreen
  <dbl> <chr>    <dbl>  <dbl>
1     1 circle   0.417  0.2  
2     1 square   0.417  0.3  
3     1 triangle 0.167  0.75 
4     2 circle   0.292  0.286
5     2 square   0.292  0.571
6     2 triangle 0.417  0.8  



Rate_green = sum ( prop_shape_i * prop_green_i )

title: 2 and 3 factor decomposition
text:
dgnpop(x = mydat,
       pop = "pop",
       factors = c("pshp","pgreen"),
       id_vars = c("shape"),
       ratefunction = "sum(pshp*pgreen)") |>
  dg_table()


DG decomposition being conducted with R = sum(pshp*pgreen)


          1    2 diff decomp
pshp   0.41 0.52 0.11     44
pgreen 0.40 0.54 0.14     56
crude  0.33 0.58 0.25    100

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text:
Whence uncertainty?

The Das Gupta method doesn’t really have a concept of an error term - all results are calculate algebraically (Das Gupta 1993)
But we might still want to quantify uncertainty in the results of a standardization and decomposition
Bootstrapping is a general purpose way to achieve this (Carsey and Harden 2014)

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text:
Bootstrapping

There are different ways to achieve this
The way we do it rate decomposition essentially expands the data out to unit-level data, re-samples, then aggregates back up
Pros: this will always* work, makes no assumptions about distributions or anything
Cons: the amount of memory required scales with the number of units, your computer might run out of memory/catch on fire

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text:
Bootstrapping in practice

First create a function to essentially “uncount” data into individual level data (where nrow = size of total population), then re-sample with replacement, then aggregate back up to the level of the original data
Standardize and decompose each resampled dataset

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text: Bootstrapping in practice

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text:
Writing an R function

If you have not written an R function before you might want to check out the Functions chapter in R for Data Science (Wickham, Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Grolemund 2023)

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text:
Bootstrapping

Now we can run our getBS function as many times as we like to get our resampled data

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text:
Bootstrapping

And now we’ve got 500 reshuffled versions of our data we can standardize and decompose each one

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text:
Bootstrapping in practice

These are the original results



                 female male   diff decomp
age_group_struct     35   37  1.928   68.9
ethnicity_struct     36   36 -0.039   -1.4
rate                 35   36  0.908   32.5
crude                35   37  2.796  100.0

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text:
Bootstrapping in practice

Now we can calculate standard errors for these estimates



# A tibble: 4 × 2
  factor              se
  <chr>            <dbl>
1 age_group_struct 0.302
2 crude            1.26 
3 ethnicity_struct 0.331
4 rate             1.27

title: Standardization and decomposition: A conceptual introduction
text:
Bootstrapping in practice

Or directly calculate confidence intervals by taking percentiles



# A tibble: 4 × 2
  factor              se
  <chr>            <dbl>
1 age_group_struct 0.302
2 crude            1.26 
3 ethnicity_struct 0.331
4 rate             1.27 






Carsey, Thomas M., and Jeffrey J. Harden. 2014. Monte Carlo Simulation and Resampling Methods for Social Science. SAGE Publications, Inc. https://doi.org/10.4135/9781483319605.


Das Gupta, Prithwis. 1993. Standardization and Decomposition of Rates: A User’s Manual.


Wickham, Hadley, Mine Çetinkaya-Rundel, and Garrett Grolemund. 2023. R for Data Science (2e). Second.
